title: Singularities of Teichmueller harmonic map flow
speaker: Melanie Rupflin
abstract: Abstract: We discuss singularities of Teichmueller harmonic map flow, which is a geometric flow that changes maps from surfaces into branched minimal immersions, and explain in particular how winding singularities of the map component can lead to singular behaviour of the metric component.
tags: Workshop on Geometric Functionals: Analysis and Applications

proof_stages.label: Dirichlet-energy formulation of minimal surfaces
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up maps from a surface into a Riemannian target and explains that critical points of the Dirichlet energy in both map and domain metric are exactly harmonic and weakly conformal maps, hence branched minimal immersions or constants.

proof_stages.label: Gauge-fixed Teichmüller harmonic map flow
proof_stages.summary: By using conformal invariance and quotienting diffeomorphism symmetries, the metric evolution is projected onto holomorphic quadratic differentials, yielding the Teichmüller harmonic map flow with better regularity than the naïve gradient flow.

proof_stages.label: Non-collapsing regime and standard bubbling
proof_stages.summary: When the injectivity radius of the domain stays bounded below, L1-control of the Hopf differential gives Ck-control of the metric evolution, so the flow behaves like classical harmonic map flow with only finitely many bubble singularities and subsequential convergence to a minimal limit at infinite time.

proof_stages.label: Degenerating collars and infinite-time loss accounting
proof_stages.summary: If the injectivity radius tends to zero, short hyperbolic geodesics create long collars and punctured limiting surfaces; at infinite time the thick part converges to minimal maps while the degenerating collars can lose only bubbles and curve-like low-energy pieces.

proof_stages.label: Finite-time singular limits and restart
proof_stages.summary: For finite-time collapse in higher genus or cylinder settings, the flow has a sequence-independent limit on the thick part and a controlled thin-part description, allowing a unique global weak solution that restarts after singular times and loses only minimal-surface pieces.

proof_stages.label: Length-evolution mechanism for finite collapse
proof_stages.summary: The speaker analyzes the evolution of a short geodesic length via a collar integral of the Hopf differential, showing that no-bubble targets prevent finite collapse while low-energy curve regions, rather than near-conformal bubble regions, can drive degeneration.

proof_stages.label: Long-image-curve criterion and threshold results
proof_stages.summary: A theorem with Robertson states that sufficiently stretched image curves on a short collar force finite-time degeneration, and a related rescaled flow has a logarithmic threshold separating nondegeneration from finite singularity.

examples.name: Sphere and torus reductions
examples.note: The speaker checks that for domain S^2 the projected metric equation vanishes and for the torus the flow reduces to harmonic map flow coupled to two ODEs, making the general definition concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the flow equations to the sphere and torus cases, using the holomorphic quadratic differential space to derive that the sphere gives harmonic map flow and the torus gives harmonic map flow plus two ODEs.

passes.reason: Sphere and torus are low-genus boundary cases with trivial or integrable Teichmüller directions and unusually large conformal symmetry, used only as reductions to known flows.

examples.name: Long cylinder in R^3 spanning distant boundary curves
examples.note: A cylinder with boundary curves too far apart to span a minimal cylinder illustrates why thin collars may limit to two minimal disks joined by a one-dimensional curve.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker draws and reasons through a long cylinder in R^3 with distant boundary curves, identifying which cylinder regions parameterize the end discs and the middle line segment.

passes.reason: The large-modulus cylinder has its middle image collapse to a line and distant boundary curves obstruct a spanning minimal cylinder, showing curve loss is necessary.

examples.name: Soft-obstacle metric producing a middle bubble
examples.note: Replacing Euclidean space by a weighted metric with a high-density region illustrates how a degenerating cylinder can lose both low-energy curve pieces and a minimal-sphere bubble.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker modifies the R^3 metric with a high-density soft obstacle and traces the resulting configuration with a central bubble, low-energy curve regions, and end discs.

examples.name: Warped-product target forcing stretched image curves
examples.note: Crossing the earlier target with an R-factor carrying a decreasing warped product metric demonstrates a setting where the long-curve degeneration criterion can actually be applied.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker constructs a warped-product target and explains how the decreasing warping factor forces the image curves to stretch so the degeneration criterion applies.

passes.reason: The warped-product target is engineered so image curves exceed the long-curve degeneration threshold, giving an adversarial example of finite-time metric degeneration.
The warped-product target forces image-curve lengths past a degeneration threshold, giving finite-time degeneration against the conjectural impossibility of such singularities.
